description: A hand meets a hot pan and 'sai' is out before a full sentence arrives. It expresses sudden physical pain and can also report an emotional sting. The cry tells others that something hurts; whether help is wanted belongs to what follows.
articulatory_notes: Raise the tongue blade near the alveolar ridge and send unvoiced air through its central groove for 's', then lower the jaw into open central 'a'. The tongue rises into high-front unrounded 'i' for the separate second syllable. Stress 'sa'.
sound_symbolism: The hiss of 's' opens into broad 'a' and narrows toward high 'i', a quick contour suggestive of the acute edge of sudden pain.
usage_notes: 'halu' (aww) responds to another person's hurt or tenderness instead of reporting the speaker's own pain.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Before blame or diagnosis, 'sai' reports pain as a fact others can respond to.
